channel.title: Whamcloud Community JIRA
channel.description: This file is an XML representation of an issue

item.title: [LU-19921] Lustre IAM integration
item.description: Proposal to implement lustre IAM integration. Please check the HLD in the attachment. Any suggestions are appreciated.

status.description: The issue is open and ready for the assignee to start work on it.

comments.comment:

Subject: LU-19921 llite: add upcall chain for md path
Project: fs/lustre-release
Branch: master
Current Patch Set: 1
Commit: 08e2fc08fbe17aac1de76d199dd75a63d7aebedc

>ddiwang could you please try again? it should be fixed now, thanks.

Ah, it works now. I simplify the document a bit. Please take a look. Thanks! Sebastien
ddiwang could you please try again? it should be fixed now, thanks.

Thanks! Sebastien.  I can create the page now. ( https://wiki.whamcloud.com/display/PUB/Lustre+mount+IAM+integration+HLD )though I still do not have the permission to upload the picture and diagram. Could you please check?
ddiwang could you please try again to create a new page on the Whamcloud wiki? All external users with a valid account should now have permission to do so.

>Hi Wang Di, let me sort this out. Are you logged in on the wiki, and what is your login name?

Hi, Sebastien, my username is ddiwang thanks!
Hi Wang Di, let me sort this out. Are you logged in on the wiki, and what is your login name?

>Would would mind uploading it to the Whamcloud wiki, under the Lustre Design documents section:

Thanks for reminding.  Sebastien. Though it seems I do not have necessary permission to create a new page under whamcloud wiki.

Hi ddiwang,

Thanks for sharing this design document. Would would mind uploading it to the Whamcloud wiki, under the Lustre Design documents section:
https://wiki.whamcloud.com/display/PUB/Lustre+Design+documents
That would make it easier for others to comment and discuss specific points. Hopefully there is an 'import document' tool, so it should be pretty straightforward.

Hmm, this feature provides 2 interfaces for IAM

  1. Client upcall to get the token (like lgss_keyring).
  2. Server upcall to authenticate the token like lsvcgss.

We will implement those GCP IAM specific details inside these two user-space upcalls. I do not have enough knowledge about other providers, though I assume others IAM providers may just need implement these upcalls to use this feature.  Please let me know if it needs other interfaces. thanks.

And yes, for testing purpose, we can implement some user-level stub for these 2 upcalls and fake some tokens to let server respond. Thanks.

I understand the motivation and it seems reasonable. My main question is unanswered though: how would you use this without GCP IAM? We'd need to be able to test this and we'd want an implementation that's reasonable vendor agnostic. I suppose we could create some kind of userspace stub to respond to the server upcall with test data. But I think we should ensure the design can accommodate open-source IAM providers. And specifically identify one that we can use to test the end-to-end solution.

Tim, Thanks for reviewing. Yes, this feature is designed to integrate Lustre mount directory access control with GCP IAM.

We initially considered using nodemaps, but that requires each client to identify its group affiliation via its NIC (or something similar) so the server can locate the correct nodemap to enforce directory access control during mount. However, determining group affiliation is difficult in a cloud environment, because

  • Multiple containers on the same VM host, belonging to different groups, share the same IP address.
  • It is almost impossible to determine group affiliation (project, org, etc.) based purely on the user, especially since a user might hold multiple roles across different groups.

Therefore, instead of relying on nodemaps, this feature integrates Google Cloud IAM directly into the Lustre mount process. During the mount, it will make an upcall to the GCP IAM server to authenticate and authorize access to the specific mount subdirectory.

The implementation leverages the existing GSS framework. An init context RPC (for IAM security) handles the authentication and authorization via the GCP IAM server. The workflow is as follows:

Client-Side Mechanics

  • Upcall Cache: The client uses an upcall cache (iam_upcall_cache) to retrieve IAM tokens for a given principal. When a user accesses the mount, the kernel calls out to a userspace helper script to fetch the user's IAM token. This token is passed back to the kernel, which then packs the token and the target mount directory into the SEC_CTX_INIT payload.

Server-Side Mechanics (MDT / OFD)

  • Token Validation: When the server receives the SEC_CTX_INIT request, it triggers a server-side upcall to validate the token and mount directory against the GCP IAM server. The IAM server returns the resulting permissions (e.g., rw, deny, expired).
  • Security Context: Upon successful validation, the server generates a security context, assigns a random 64-bit handle to it, and replies to the client with this context.

Client Connection

  • Connection Verification: The client then sends a connect RPC to the server that includes this 64-bit handle. The server uses the handle to locate the corresponding security context and attaches it to the export. All subsequent RPCs will be verified against this security context.

Sebastien Buisson is probably the best person to review this. But at a glance, this implementation seems to depend on some unamed third party IAM implementation, presumably something GCP? I don't think the document does enough to explain what the server implementation would look like and how someone outside of GCP would leverage this functionality. Do you envision this as a strict superset of the existing Kerberos integration? Some examples along those lines would be helpful for those not familiar with GCP (or cloud in general). And it could help outline what a possible sanity testing suite could look like.
